title: Authentic Hadith Collections Online: Sahih Bukhari, Sahih Muslim and the Six Books
excerpt: Access the most trusted Hadith collections in the world — Sahih Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, Nasa'i, and Ibn Majah — with English translations and authenticity grades. Understand the collection, the collectors, and why authenticity matters.
author: QuranBookk
content:
## He Reviewed 600,000 Narrations and Kept 7,563

In approximately 216 AH (830 CE), a 16-year-old scholar from Bukhara began a journey that would take him across the Islamic world — Iraq, Hijaz, Syria, Egypt — in search of the narrations of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. His name was Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari.

For over 16 years, he collected, examined, and scrutinised every hadith he could reach. He evaluated not just the text of each narration but the character, memory, reliability, and connection of every single person in its chain of transmission — *isnad*. His methodology: accept a narration only when every narrator in the chain was known to be of impeccable character and memory, and when it could be confirmed that each narrator had actually met the next in the chain.

Of the approximately 600,000 narrations he reviewed, he kept **7,563** — including repetitions, approximately 2,602 unique hadiths. He rejected 592,437.

Sahih al-Bukhari is the result: considered by the consensus of Islamic scholarship to be the most authentic book in existence after the Quran itself.

The [QuranBookk Authentic Hadiths collection](/hadiths/hadiths-sources/) makes the complete text of Sahih Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the other major collections accessible in English and Arabic — free, from any browser.

## The Six Canonical Hadith Collections (Kutub al-Sittah)

Islamic scholarship recognises six primary Hadith collections as the foundational references for the Prophetic Sunnah:

### 1. Sahih al-Bukhari — Imam al-Bukhari (d. 256 AH)
The most rigorous hadith collection in existence. Al-Bukhari's criterion was the highest: every narrator must have met the next in the chain, and this must be verifiable. The collection covers Islamic law, worship, character, history, and the Prophet's ﷺ daily practice across 97 chapters. For any Islamic ruling or practice, the first reference is Bukhari.

### 2. Sahih Muslim — Imam Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj (d. 261 AH)
The second most authoritative collection, often paired with Bukhari as *Al-Sahihayn* (The Two Sahihs). Muslim's methodology was slightly different from Bukhari's — he required that narrators in the chain *could have met* each other, not necessarily that they provably did. This difference in methodology produces occasional differences in the hadiths each includes. Muslim's organisation by subject matter (without repeating the same hadith in multiple chapters as Bukhari does) makes it particularly useful for thematic study.

### 3. Sunan Abu Dawud — Imam Abu Dawud al-Sijistani (d. 275 AH)
Abu Dawud collected 500,000 hadiths and kept approximately 4,800 — selected specifically for their relevance to Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh). This makes Sunan Abu Dawud the primary hadith reference for practical legal questions. He is said to have stated that he included some slightly weaker hadiths in legal matters when no stronger narration existed on the topic.

### 4. Jami' al-Tirmidhi — Imam al-Tirmidhi (d. 279 AH)
Distinguished from the other collections by its inclusion of the scholars' opinions on each hadith — Tirmidhi often notes whether scholars considered it authentic, good, weak, or disputed. This makes it an invaluable reference for understanding the scholarly discourse around specific narrations. Many widely quoted hadiths come from Tirmidhi.

### 5. Sunan al-Nasa'i — Imam al-Nasa'i (d. 303 AH)
Considered by many scholars to have the most stringent criterion after Bukhari and Muslim. Al-Nasa'i's collection is particularly valuable for hadiths related to prayer (Salah) — his chapter on the details of prayer is among the most detailed in hadith literature.

### 6. Sunan Ibn Majah — Imam Ibn Majah (d. 273 AH)
The sixth of the canonical collections. Contains approximately 4,341 hadiths, including some narrations not found in the other five collections. Scholars note that Ibn Majah's collection contains a higher proportion of weak hadiths than the other five, making it important to check authenticity grades carefully for narrations found only in Ibn Majah.

## Understanding Hadith Authenticity Grades

Not all hadiths are equal. The scholars of Hadith developed a rigorous grading system based on the quality of the chain of transmission and the text:

**Sahih (صَحِيح) — Authentic**
The highest grade. A continuous chain of narrators, each of whom is known to be upright (*adl*) and of strong memory (*dhabt*), with no defect in the chain or contradictions in the text. Hadiths from Bukhari and Muslim are by default considered Sahih.

**Hasan (حَسَن) — Good**
Meets most of Sahih criteria but with a slight weakness in a narrator's memory — not defective but not as strong. Hasan hadiths are used in Islamic law and practice. Much of Tirmidhi and Abu Dawud falls into this category.

**Da'if (ضَعِيف) — Weak**
Contains a defect — a break in the chain, an unknown narrator, a narrator of poor memory, or contradictions with stronger narrations. Scholars differ on whether Da'if hadiths can be used for anything. Most allow them only for virtuous deeds (*fada'il al-a'mal*), not for obligatory practices or rulings.

**Mawdu' (مَوْضُوع) — Fabricated**
A narration proven to have been invented — either through confession by a narrator, an impossible chain, or clear contradictions with the Quran and established Sunnah. Using a Mawdu' hadith as religious evidence is a serious matter.

<Callout type="tip">
When sharing a hadith — on social media, in a lesson, or in conversation — always check its authenticity grade. "Seek knowledge even unto China" — widely shared, authenticity disputed. "The ink of the scholar is holier than the blood of the martyr" — not established as an authentic hadith. Verification before sharing is an Islamic obligation. The [Hadith AI](/ask-hadith-ai/) can help check authenticity for specific narrations.
</Callout>

## Why Accessing Primary Sources Directly Matters

The internet has produced a hadith misquotation crisis. Fabricated hadiths, weak hadiths presented as authentic, and partial hadiths presented without context circulate constantly. The only reliable verification method is checking the primary collections directly.

The [QuranBookk Authentic Hadiths collection](/hadiths/hadiths-sources/) provides direct access to the source texts — allowing any Muslim to verify whether a hadith appears in Bukhari, Muslim, or the other collections, with its reference number and English translation.

For targeted searching — finding hadiths on a specific topic across all collections — pair the Hadiths collection with [Ask Hadith AI](/ask-hadith-ai/), which can search across collections and provide references for you to verify in the primary source.

You can also explore the Sunnah further through the [Random Hadith feature](/hadiths/random-hadith/bukhari) — which surfaces a random authentic narration from Sahih Bukhari with each visit, connecting you to parts of the Prophetic legacy you may never have encountered.

## The Six Books — Online, Free, With English Translation

The [QuranBookk Authentic Hadiths collection](/hadiths/hadiths-sources/) provides access to the canonical hadith collections — free, no account required, accessible from any browser. The same collections that scholars have studied for over a thousand years, available in your browser now.
